textDisplay: Just watched Call of the wild and man did it ever touch my heart. Fantastic acting and great story also thought they did a good job on the animating of the movie. Harrison Ford did a great job acting, it really made me appreciate his career in acting and how lucky I am to have seen his career in movies. I guess that was a bit off topic but I just don't know how many years he has left and there's just no actor who could ever take his place. Makes me kind of want to take up acting just so that I could learn his tricks just so that the joy he bring in movies could be past on. I'll support his movies till the end. So at the end I did really really like the movie but on the side note not any other actor could of filled his roll.

textDisplay:
Trying to read thru some of the comments to get an idea of what’s good or bad about the movie. 
As I waded through I realized what a part of our society has become. Most everyone commenting is focused on trying to come up w some clever single line to take down the movie. And I do include the words trying to. That hits me as sad. I hope some time in the not too distant future society trends away from that cuz it’s really lame, hallow and one dimensional.

textDisplay: I can see using cgi in abusive scenes, and otherwise minimally if it radically enhances the quality of the movie. But this usage with such a classic story takes all of the air out. A current adaptation should be an improvement over previous ones, but the Charleton Heston version still has this one beat by a mile. It's too bad, Harrison Ford seems to have so much more potential, yet continues to come across as arrogant, condescending, entititled and egotistical with alcoholic tendances (that's probably just Hollywood's booze-pushing relationship with the alcohol industry). I don't mean to be too critical of HF, he's succeeded in many top rolls, but I think he's got more to offer.
textOriginal: I can see using cgi in abusive scenes, and otherwise minimally if it radically enhances the quality of the movie. But this usage with such a classic story takes all of the air out. A current adaptation should be an improvement over previous ones, but the Charleton Heston version still has this one beat by a mile. It's too bad, Harrison Ford seems to have so much more potential, yet continues to come across as arrogant, condescending, entititled and egotistical with alcoholic tendances (that's probably just Hollywood's booze-pushing relationship with the alcohol industry). I don't mean to be too critical of HF, he's succeeded in many top rolls, but I think he's got more to offer.

textDisplay: I read “Call of the Wild” as a child, and it was a wonderful tale that made an emotional impact. Now Hollywood gets a hold of it and turns into a cheesy, gaudy display of CGI. A team of sled dogs suddenly looks like they should be firing lasers from their heads in the next Star Wars film. I’ll stick to watching any of the early Black and White movies from the 50s or made for TV movies of the 70s & 80s over this special effects laden rubbish.
textOriginal: I read “Call of the Wild” as a child, and it was a wonderful tale that made an emotional impact. Now Hollywood gets a hold of it and turns into a cheesy, gaudy display of CGI. A team of sled dogs suddenly looks like they should be firing lasers from their heads in the next Star Wars film. I’ll stick to watching any of the early Black and White movies from the 50s or made for TV movies of the 70s & 80s over this special effects laden rubbish.

textDisplay: I don't... there's no way this movie can have even a shred of accuracy based on this trailer. Is Harrison Ford supposed to be the last owner? He was only around for the last little bit of the book. He had no clue of Buck's previous life. He basically nursed him back to health then did the sled challenge, that's it. And don't even get me started on Buck, who is supposed to be way more like a wolf. Granted it's been a few years since I've read it, but Call of the Wild was by far one of my all-time favorites; yet another book to add to the collection of being butchered by Hollywood film.
textOriginal: I don't... there's no way this movie can have even a shred of accuracy based on this trailer. Is Harrison Ford supposed to be the last owner? He was only around for the last little bit of the book. He had no clue of Buck's previous life. He basically nursed him back to health then did the sled challenge, that's it. And don't even get me started on Buck, who is supposed to be way more like a wolf. Granted it's been a few years since I've read it, but Call of the Wild was by far one of my all-time favorites; yet another book to add to the collection of being butchered by Hollywood film.

textDisplay: You can sooo easily tell the hallmark touches of a Hollywood bs film as the people with the power so desperately want to recoup the investment in the film that they rely on star recognition, effects, and cliche over the top writing.  Then there's the profit ratios based on marketing.  How much to spend on marketing vs how many will be convinced by the marketing to see this.  It's so obvious.  The best movies are never marketed BECAUSE they took a chance on making the film in the first place!!  No chances were taken here.  Making money is everything.

textDisplay:
The Call of the Wild was the first book I really fell in love with, like back before I was even a teenager. I just.... can't find it in me to be drawn to this movie in any way. I think I just feel too jaded by movies in general lately, so I feel especially distrustful when I see it happening to something I love. Which would be a shame if this one turns out to be good.

Still, where's the man in the red sweater? Where's Spitz? Why POV from what I assume is John Thornton if he's eventually just gonna....? And who's that woman? Only woman I remember from the book who went through the ice.... well....

Ugh, tell me she's not there just to fill some stupid quota....
textOriginal:
The Call of the Wild was the first book I really fell in love with, like back before I was even a teenager. I just.... can't find it in me to be drawn to this movie in any way. I think I just feel too jaded by movies in general lately, so I feel especially distrustful when I see it happening to something I love. Which would be a shame if this one turns out to be good.

Still, where's the man in the red sweater? Where's Spitz? Why POV from what I assume is John Thornton if he's eventually just gonna....? And who's that woman? Only woman I remember from the book who went through the ice.... well....

Ugh, tell me she's not there just to fill some stupid quota....

textDisplay: I understand why they used CGI. It's not perfect, but it looks good enough. And, no, it's not truly faithful to the book but few movies are ever completely faithful to a 350-page book. Books have much more involved plots, and it's impossible to get all that into a 90-page screenplay. You have to cut a lot of stuff, combine stuff, and there is also the current political climate. What worked well in a novel written almost seventy years ago (a world away) isn't necessarily going to work in our current cultural climate. But the story remains a classic. Even with adaptations. So, overall, it looks like a good movie. I'll definitely see it.
textOriginal: I understand why they used CGI. It's not perfect, but it looks good enough. And, no, it's not truly faithful to the book but few movies are ever completely faithful to a 350-page book. Books have much more involved plots, and it's impossible to get all that into a 90-page screenplay. You have to cut a lot of stuff, combine stuff, and there is also the current political climate. What worked well in a novel written almost seventy years ago (a world away) isn't necessarily going to work in our current cultural climate. But the story remains a classic. Even with adaptations. So, overall, it looks like a good movie. I'll definitely see it.

textDisplay: In my honest opinion, THIS MOVIE IS BEST SUITED TO BE MADE IN ANIMATED FORMAT!!! In 3D CGI!!! Although 2D would have been much more better, but alas, that medium format has long since gone the way of the dinosaurs. Also, it could’ve been a companion piece to Netflix’s White Fang. So yeah, my way of saying that I have my doubts about this movie in this matter, what with its use of CGI (e.g. Buck and the rest of the dogs), but what can you do? Best to go see it for myself when it’s out and I’ll make my judgements from there. Maybe it’ll be good (God willing 😣🙏🏾), maybe not (God forbid 🙅🏾‍♂️)). Let’s see about that, come that time 😌🤷🏾‍♂️
textOriginal: In my honest opinion, THIS MOVIE IS BEST SUITED TO BE MADE IN ANIMATED FORMAT!!! In 3D CGI!!! textDisplay: Why do studios keep making movies that are just total CGI fests? That avalanche doesn't look real (therefore my brain doesn't feel real tension), the dogs don't look real (therefore my brain doesn't invest in the fake characters), and frankly, little else in this trailer looks real. Hey, producers and executives: Just because you can, doesn't mean you should.
textOriginal: Why do studios keep making movies that are just total CGI fests? That avalanche doesn't look real (therefore my brain doesn't feel real tension), the dogs don't look real (therefore my brain doesn't invest in the fake characters), and frankly, little else in this trailer looks real. Hey, producers and executives: Just because you can, doesn't mean you should.

textDisplay:
Siedler Joe
You completely missed the point. 

What everyone is saying is that movie adaptations of books are never as good as the original book. Nobody is saying the Movie is "too tame" or whatever. What people are saying is that films like this have a bad track record. Honestly, book adaptations just don't work as movies. Eragon, Harry Potter, Lord of the Rings, etc.  Nobody wants to re-enact every moment of the film, they just don't want the book to be ruined.

As for the CGI, it does look stupid. Why couldn't they just use real dogs? Obviously for stunts and whatever they could use CGI, but for the whole movie? That's just dumb.

You sound like you have some serious self confidence issues, with your whole "hyper criticism thing." Maybe you feel criticized a lot? Ikd man, maybe get that checked out.

As for the CGI, it does look stupid. Why couldn't they just use real dogs? Obviously for stunts and whatever they could use CGI, but for the whole movie? That's just dumb.

You sound like you have some serious self confidence issues, with your whole "hyper criticism thing." Maybe you feel criticized a lot? Ikd man, maybe get that checked out.

textDisplay:
Hmmm... I’m certainly interested, and Harrison Ford is a great choice. However, this looks a little, for lack of a better word, Disney-fied for me (which I guess is fitting, given how the studio is now owned by Disney). I know the novel is ok for younger readers, but it’s also pretty dark and violent, especially at the end. This seems just a little too whimsical to end on such a note without some tonal inconsistency.

I guess we’ll see when the movie comes out, though.
textOriginal:
Hmmm... I’m certainly interested, and Harrison Ford is a great choice. However, this looks a little, for lack of a better word, Disney-fied for me (which I guess is fitting, given how the studio is now owned by Disney). I know the novel is ok for younger readers, but it’s also pretty dark and violent, especially at the end. This seems just a little too whimsical to end on such a note without some tonal inconsistency.

I guess we’ll see when the movie comes out, though.
